articles.title: Is there still room for conservative treatment of Distal Biceps Tendon ruptures? What patients (and surgeons) should know. A case series with a mean 4-years follow-up.
articles.authors: Coliva F, Marinelli A, Riva M, Valentina V, Tortorella F, Minerba M, et al.
articles.population: Patients with distal biceps tendon rupture treated conservatively
articles.outcomes: Strength deficits (supination 11%, flexion 4-5%), DASH score 10.1, MEPS score 97.5, pain during repetitive activities in 22.5%
articles.take_home: Conservative treatment for distal biceps tendon rupture works reasonably well for sedentary patients with only small strength losses, but patients doing heavy repetitive work experience more pain and poorer function, so surgery may be better for them. Chronic pain is a common problem that patients should be aware of when considering non-surgical treatment.

articles.title: Propensity Matched Professional Baseball Pitchers With Early Trunk Rotation Demonstrate Increased Shoulder and Elbow Distractive Forces with Equivalent Ball Velocity.
articles.authors: Manzi J, Dillon M, Dowling B, Eskew J, Patel S, Kunze K, et al.
articles.sample_size: 339 pitchers evaluated; 33 early trunk rotation pitchers matched to 132 late trunk rotation pitchers (132 matched pairs)
articles.outcomes: Throwing arm kinetics, shoulder and elbow distractive forces, ball velocity, pelvic and trunk mechanics, prior upper-extremity injury prevalence
articles.take_home: Professional pitchers who started rotating their trunk before their front foot touched down showed increased stress on their shoulder and elbow joints but threw the ball at the same speed as those who waited until foot contact. This suggests their bodies were working harder to achieve the same result, which may not be an efficient technique.

articles.title: All-Suture Anchors Exhibit Less Perianchor Fluid Formation Independent of Healing Quality Compared With PEEK Anchors in Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair: A Propensity Score-Matched Comparative Study.
articles.authors: Gul O, Ayazoglu MA, Yazici A, Durusoy S, Gurun E, Okutan AE
articles.study_type: Retrospective comparative study with propensity score matching
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with either all-suture anchors or PEEK anchors
articles.outcomes: Clinical outcomes (pain, shoulder function, range of motion), tendon healing rates, perianchor fluid collection on MRI
articles.take_home: All-suture anchors and PEEK anchors produce similar clinical results and healing rates, but all-suture anchors cause less fluid collection around the anchor site. Fluid collection around PEEK anchors was linked to worse healing, but this connection was not seen with all-suture anchors.

articles.title: Towards a common definition of frozen shoulder: a scoping review of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Karasuyama M, Ishikawa H, Uchida T, Saka M, Muraki T, Minamikawa T, et al.
articles.study_type: Scoping review of randomised controlled trials
articles.population: Adults with frozen shoulder or adhesive capsulitis
articles.sample_size: 310 randomised controlled trials included from 4,426 records identified
articles.outcomes: How frozen shoulder is defined across trials, including terminology, age criteria, pain characteristics, range of motion limitations, symptom duration, and imaging requirements
articles.take_home: Frozen shoulder is defined very differently across research studies, which makes it hard to compare results between trials. Standardised definitions for terminology, pain levels, movement limitations, symptom duration, and imaging would help make future research more reliable and easier to understand.

articles.title: Temporal trends and estimated lifetime attributable radiation risk of preoperative planning computed tomography for primary shoulder arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Kalva SR, Fucich D, Perry AJ, Lezak B, Torkieh J, Joshi T, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective observational study of radiation exposure trends
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary shoulder arthroplasty between 2016 and 2024
articles.outcomes: Annual percentage of patients receiving preoperative planning CT scans; lifetime attributable risk of cancer stratified by sex, age, and cancer type
articles.take_home: The use of preoperative CT scans for shoulder surgery has increased from 53% to 66% of patients over the study period. Whilst the cancer risk from a single CT scan is small, younger women may have a slightly higher lifetime cancer risk from the radiation exposure compared to older patients and men.

articles.title: Shoulder Muscle Adiposity Can Predict the Efficacy of Corticosteroid Injections in Treating Adhesive Capsulitis After Rotator Cuff Repair Surgery.
articles.authors: Chen NH, Lin CL, Cheng YH, Chiu YS, Hsu TH, Huang SW
articles.population: Patients who developed adhesive capsulitis (shoulder stiffness) after rotator cuff repair surgery and received corticosteroid injection
articles.outcomes: Pain scores and range of motion at 8 weeks; fatty infiltration in shoulder muscles measured on preoperative MRI using Goutallier fatty degeneration index
articles.take_home: Patients with higher levels of fatty changes in their shoulder muscles before surgery were six times more likely to have corticosteroid injections fail to treat post-surgery stiffness. This measurement on pre-surgery scans could help doctors identify patients who might need different treatments earlier.

articles.title: Is a 32 mm glenosphere optimal in reverse shoulder arthroplasty for patients with small glenoid anatomy? : a range of motion simulation study in petite Asian females.
articles.authors: Kiyomatsu H, Fujibuchi T, Kutsuna T, Kinoshita T, Tsuda T, Hayashi M, et al.
articles.population: Asian female patients with small glenoid anatomy undergoing reverse shoulder arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 11 patients with postoperative outcomes; 3D CT planning analysis performed
articles.outcomes: Impingement-free range of motion in extension-flexion, abduction-adduction, and external-internal rotation with different glenosphere diameters (32 mm, 36 mm, 39 mm) and inferior overhangs (1.5 mm, 3.0 mm, 4.5 mm)
articles.take_home: In patients with small shoulder joint anatomy, using a smaller 32 mm glenosphere with adequate positioning appears to give better movement range than larger implants. The study authors note that real-world testing in patients is still needed to confirm these computer predictions.

articles.title: Non-Opioid Pain Management in Younger Patients Undergoing Arthroscopic Glenoid Labrum Surgery: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Tornberg HN, Czachor MK, Gutowski CT, Criscione JX, Kalani CM, Dibato J, et al.
articles.population: Opioid-naive patients aged 15 to 25 years undergoing arthroscopic glenoid labrum repair
articles.outcomes: Postoperative opioid consumption in morphine milligram equivalents, visual analogue scale pain scores over 14 days, and patient satisfaction with pain control
articles.take_home: Young patients having shoulder labrum surgery can manage pain effectively without opioids using a multimodal non-opioid approach. Both groups used very little opioid pain relief, had similar pain scores, and reported high satisfaction with their pain management.

articles.title: Shoulder Arthroplasty Outcomes in Hypertensive Patients: A Propensity Score-Matched Comparison of ARBs and ACE Inhibitors.
articles.authors: Lee S, Jayne C, Kim MT, Boufadel P, Cook AE, Uppstrom TJ, et al.
articles.sample_size: 11,603 patients (5,705 in each ARB and ACEI group after matching)
articles.outcomes: Dislocation, periprosthetic fracture, stiffness, revision rates, medical and surgical complications, readmission and emergency department visits at 90 days, 1 year and 2 years
articles.take_home: Hypertensive patients taking ARB blood pressure medications had lower rates of shoulder dislocation, fracture around the implant and stiffness after shoulder replacement compared to those on ACE inhibitors. These differences suggest ARBs may offer musculoskeletal benefits, but the findings were not consistent across all types of shoulder surgery and need confirmation with further research.

articles.title: Image-Based Quantitative Osteotomy Planning for Congenital Proximal Radioulnar Synostosis: Plan-Execution Discrepancy and Postoperative Radiographic Outcomes.
articles.authors: Chen YC, Shih HJ, Chang CH, Wang TM, Yang CY, Shih PJ
articles.study_type: Retrospective case series with computational modelling validation
articles.population: Children with congenital proximal radioulnar synostosis (forearm bone fusion)
articles.outcomes: Radiographic radial head alignment, forearm rotational range of motion, plan-execution discrepancy between model predictions and actual surgical measurements
articles.take_home: A computer-based planning system can provide objective measurements to guide surgery for this rare condition, and larger discrepancies between planned and actual bone shortening were linked to poorer radial head positioning after surgery. This shows promise for reducing surgeon variability, though the small sample size means further testing is needed.

articles.title: Stepwise loss of shoulder stabilizers induces humeral head migration and progression of cuff tear arthropathy in a rat model.
articles.authors: Uekama K, Iuchi T, Ide T, Shimanoe K, Masuda Y, Takada S, et al.
articles.outcomes: Superior humeral head migration, cartilage degeneration, inflammatory markers, bone remodelling changes
articles.take_home: Damaging the rotator cuff, shoulder capsule and biceps tendon in sequence causes the upper arm bone to move upwards and triggers progressive joint damage in rats. The shoulder capsule is more important than the biceps tendon in preventing this upward movement, and repairing these structures might slow down arthritis development.

articles.title: Operative Timing Predicts Postoperative Complications after Staged Reverse Total Shoulder Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Wier J, Mayfield CK, Mouchawar AC, Barcenas AB, Weber AE, Gamradt SC, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using large healthcare database
articles.population: Patients in the United States undergoing primary reverse total shoulder arthroplasty between 2016-2020
articles.sample_size: 3,152 staged bilateral procedures matched to 9,456 unilateral procedures
articles.outcomes: 90-day surgical complications including fracture and infection
articles.take_home: Having surgery on the second shoulder within a year of the first reverse shoulder replacement significantly increases the risk of complications like fracture and infection. Waiting more than 12 months between operations reduces this risk to levels similar to having only one shoulder operated on.

articles.title: Does robust greater tuberosity repair affect the outcomes of reverse shoulder replacement in proximal humerus fractures? - A Bayesian network meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Ponugoti N, Selim A, Elhassan A, Jeavons R
articles.population: Older adults with proximal humerus fractures treated with reverse shoulder replacement
articles.outcomes: Constant score, abduction, external rotation and forward flexion
articles.take_home: Repairing the greater tuberosity bone during reverse shoulder replacement surgery improves how well the shoulder works after the operation, but only if the repair actually heals. If the repair does not heal, the result is similar to not attempting repair at all.

articles.title: MCP-1 Exacerbates Diabetic Rotator Cuff Tear by Driving Synovial Inflammation via MAPK/ERK-Mediated Macrophage Polarization.
articles.authors: Wang Y, Gu X, Tu J, Xu B
articles.study_type: Basic science study investigating molecular mechanisms in diabetic rotator cuff tears, using human tissue samples and animal models
articles.population: 30 patients with diabetes and rotator cuff tears, 30 non-diabetic controls with rotator cuff tears, and diabetic mice models
articles.sample_size: 60 human participants; animal study numbers not specified
articles.outcomes: Synovial inflammation markers, MCP-1 expression, pain thresholds, functional impairment, tendon degeneration, macrophage polarisation pathways
articles.take_home: Diabetic patients develop more severe rotator cuff tears because of increased inflammation driven by a molecule called MCP-1. Blocking this molecule in animal models reduced inflammation and pain, suggesting it could be a new treatment target for people with diabetes who have rotator cuff problems.

articles.title: The meta-analysis and systematic review of arthroscopic lower trapezius tendon transfer for massive irreparable rotator cuff tears.
articles.authors: Baek CH, Kim JG, Kim BT, Lim C
articles.population: Patients with massive irreparable posterosuperior rotator cuff tears
articles.outcomes: External rotation improved by 27.9 degrees, forward flexion by 37.0 degrees, pain reduced by 4.2 points on visual analogue scale, ASES score improved by 32.5 points, Constant score improved by 22.7 points, graft healing rate 89.4%, complications 3.70%, reoperation 3.46%, conversion to reverse shoulder replacement 2.08%
articles.take_home: Lower trapezius tendon transfer performed arthroscopically can improve shoulder movement, reduce pain, and restore function in patients with massive rotator cuff tears that cannot be repaired normally, with good graft healing and low complication rates.

articles.title: Unstable distal clavicle fractures: Outcomes of internal fixation of clavicle and coracoclavicular stabilization.
articles.authors: Xu JJ, Molokwu BO, Shabbir-Hussain R, Myerson CL, Kwon YW, Virk MS
articles.population: Patients with unstable distal clavicle fractures treated with surgery
articles.outcomes: Patient-reported outcome measurement information system scores, American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons scores, Visual Analog Scale pain scores, and complications
articles.take_home: Patients who had surgery for unstable distal clavicle fractures had good results with high satisfaction scores and low pain levels. Adding extra stabilisation with sutures or anchors to the plate fixation appeared to help achieve predictable healing.

articles.title: Routine gross pathology analysis of the humeral head after shoulder arthroplasty lacks cost-effectiveness and clinical utility.
articles.authors: Nakken ER, Megwa R, Wang Z, Aibinder WR
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total shoulder arthroplasty or hemiarthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Agreement between surgeon pre-operative diagnosis and gross pathology findings; change in patient care management; cost per discordant diagnosis
articles.take_home: Surgeons correctly identified humeral head problems 95.6% of the time, and even when pathology found something different, it never changed how patients were treated. Sending humeral heads for routine pathology testing costs money without providing clinical benefit.
